---
title: "Modules, Packages,<br/>and uv"
subtitle: "Lecture 05"
author: "Dr. Colin Rundel"
footer: "Sta 663 - Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: slides.scss
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: true
---

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, include=FALSE}
{{< include _setup.R >}}
```


# Modules

## What is a module?

In Python, a *module* is simply a file containing Python code (`.py` extension). 

Modules provide:

* A way to organize related code into separate files
* A namespace to avoid naming conflicts
* Reusability of code across projects

. . .

Any Python file can be imported as a module,

::: {.code-file .sourceCode .cell-code}
`r fontawesome::fa("file")` &nbsp; `Lec05_module.py`
:::
::: {.small}
```{python}
#| eval: false
#| file: "Lec05_module.py"
```
:::

## Importing modules

The `import` statement loads a module (i.e. runs the code) and creates a namespace for its contents,

::: {.small}
```{python}
import Lec05_module
```
:::

. . .

Contents are then accessed via the namespace using dot notation,

::: {.small}
```{python}
Lec05_module.greet("Jane")
Lec05_module.pi
```
:::

## Import variations

There are several additional ways to import modules or their contents:

::: {.small}
```{python}
import Lec05_module                       # import the module
from Lec05_module import pi               # import specific objects
from Lec05_module import greet as hello   # import with alias
import Lec05_module as module             # import module with alias
```
:::

. . .

::: {.small}
```{python}
pi
hello("John")
module.pi
```
:::

. . .

The `from module import *` pattern imports all public names but is considered bad practice as it can lead to namespace pollution and naming conflicts.


## Module search path

When you import a module, Python searches for it in the following order:

1. The current working directory directory
2. Directories in the `PYTHONPATH` environment variable
3. Standard library directories
4. Site-packages (installed third-party packages)

. . .

You can inspect the search path via `sys.path`,

::: {.xsmall}
```{python}
#| eval: false
import sys
sys.path
```
```{python}
import sys
print("[", ",\n  ".join([x for x in sys.path if x]), "]")
```
:::


## Module attributes

Modules have several built-in attributes that provide useful metadata,

::: {.columns .xsmall}
::: {.column}
```{python}
module.__name__
module.__file__
print(module.__doc__)
```
:::
::: {.column}
```{python}
import json
json.__name__
json.__file__
print(json.__doc__[:200], "...")
```
:::
:::




## `dir()` function

The `dir()` function lists all names defined in a module (or any object),

::: {.xsmall}
```{python}
dir(module)
dir(json)
```
:::

## `__name__` and `"__main__"`

Every module has a `__name__` attribute. When a module is run directly, `__name__` is set to `"__main__"`. When imported, it's set to the module's name.

::: {.code-file .sourceCode .cell-code}
`r fontawesome::fa("file")` &nbsp; `Lec05_app.py`
:::
::: {.small}
```{python}
#| eval: false
#| file: Lec05_app.py
```
:::

. . .

::: {.columns .small} 
::: {.column}
```{bash}
uv run Lec05_app.py
```
:::
::: {.column}
```{python}
from Lec05_app import  main
main()
```
:::
:::



# Packages

## What is a package?

A *package* is a way to organize related modules into a hierarchal structure using directories. A package is a directory containing:

* An `__init__.py` file (can be empty)
* One or more module files (`.py`)
* Optionally, sub-packages (subdirectories with their own `__init__.py`)

. . .

```
data_tools/
    __init__.py
    io/
        __init__.py
        csv_reader.py
        json_reader.py
    analysis/
        __init__.py
        statistics.py
        visualization.py
    utils.py
```

## `__init__.py`

The `__init__.py` file:

* Marks a directory as a Python package
* Runs when the package is imported
* Can be empty or contain initialization code
* Can define `__all__` to control `from package import *`

. . .

::: {.code-file .sourceCode .cell-code}
`r fontawesome::fa("file")` &nbsp; `data_tools/__init__.py`
:::
::: {.small}
```{python}
#| eval: false
from .utils import helper_func
from .io.csv_reader import read_csv

__all__ = ["helper_func", "read_csv"]
```
:::

::: {.aside}
As of Python 3.3, `__init__.py` is technically optional for "namespace packages", but it's still recommended for regular packages.
:::


## httpx - `__init__.py` example

::: {.xsmall}
```{python}
#| eval: false
from .__version__ import __version__

from ._api import delete, get, head, options, patch, post, put, request, stream
from ._auth import BasicAuth, DigestAuth, NetRCAuth
from ._client import AsyncClient, Client
from ._config import Limits, Proxy, Timeout, create_ssl_context
from ._exceptions import (ConnectError, ConnectTimeout, HTTPError, HTTPStatusError, ...)
from ._models import Cookies, Headers, Request, Response
from ._transports import AsyncHTTPTransport, HTTPTransport, MockTransport
from ._urls import URL, QueryParams

__all__ = [
    "__version__",
    "AsyncClient", "Client",
    "delete", "get", "head", "options", "patch", "post", "put", "request",
    "BasicAuth", "DigestAuth", "NetRCAuth",
    "Limits", "Proxy", "Timeout",
    "HTTPError", "ConnectError", "TimeoutException",
    "Request", "Response", "Headers", "Cookies", "URL",
    ...
]
```
:::

::: {.aside}
Adapted from [encode/httpx/](https://github.com/encode/httpx/blob/master/httpx/__init__.py)
:::


## Importing from packages

Packages allow for hierarchical imports,

::: {.small}
```{python}
from collections import Counter
Counter("mississippi")
```
:::

. . .

::: {.small}
```{python}
from urllib.parse import urlparse
urlparse("https://example.com/path?query=1")
```
:::


## The standard library

Python comes with a large collection of built-in packages called the *standard library* - these are available without installing anything extra.

. . .

Some useful examples:

:::: {.columns .small}
::: {.column width='50%'}
* `json` - JSON encoding/decoding
* `pathlib` - filesystem paths
* `os` - operating system interface
* `sys` - system-specific parameters
* `re` - regular expressions
* `datetime` - dates and times
:::

::: {.column width='50%'}
* `collections` - specialized containers
* `itertools` - iteration utilities
* `functools` - higher-order functions
* `math` - mathematical functions
* `random` - random number generation
* `typing` - type hints
:::
::::

::: {.aside}
See the full list: [Python Standard Library](https://docs.python.org/3/library/index.html)
:::


## Exercise 1

Given the following package structure:

```
analytics/
    __init__.py
    data/
        __init__.py
        loader.py      # contains: load_csv(), load_json()
        cleaner.py     # contains: remove_nulls(), normalize()
    models/
        __init__.py
        regression.py  # contains: LinearModel class
```

Write import statements to:

1. Import `load_csv` from `loader.py`
2. Import both functions from `cleaner.py`
3. Import `LinearModel` with the alias `lm`
4. From within `regression.py`, import `remove_nulls` using a relative import


# Package Management <br/>with uv

## Python packaging landscape

Python's packaging ecosystem has historically been fragmented:

* Multiple tools: `pip`, `virtualenv`, `venv`, `conda`, `poetry`, `pipenv`, etc.
* Multiple config files: `requirements.txt`, `setup.py`, `pyproject.toml`, etc.
* Version management often handled separately (`pyenv`)

. . .

**uv** is a modern tool that aims to unify these concerns with a fast, Rust-based implementation.


## What is uv?

**uv** is a Python package and project manager developed by [Astral](https://astral.sh) (creators of `ruff`).

<br/>

Key features:

* Extremely fast (10-100x faster than pip)
* Manages Python versions
* Creates and manages virtual environments
* Installs packages
* Handles project dependencies via `pyproject.toml`
* Drop-in replacement for `pip` and `virtualenv`
* Directly supported by Positron and Reticulate

## Installing uv


On MacOS/Linux:

::: {.small}
```bash
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
```
:::

or with homebrew:

::: {.small}
```bash
brew install uv
```
:::

or with pip / pipx

::: {.small}
```bash
pipx install uv
pip install uc
```
:::


## Verify installation

Once installed you should be able to run the following,

```{bash}
uv --version
```

. . .

As long as you have version `0.9.*` you should be fine.



## Managing Python versions

uv can install and manage multiple Python versions,

::: {.columns .small}
::: {.column .xsmall}
```{bash}
uv python list
```
:::
::: {.column}
```{bash}
uv python install 3.14
```
```{bash}
uv python pin 3.14
```
:::
:::

. . .

The pinned version is stored in `~/.python-version` and will be used automatically.


## Initializing a project

Use `uv init` to create a new project,

::: {.small}
```bash
mkdir my-project && cd my-project
uv init
```
```
Initialized project `my-project`
```
```bash
ls
```
```
total 32
drwxr-xr-x@   8 rundel  staff   256 Jan 21 13:22 .
drwxr-xr-x@ 135 rundel  staff  4320 Jan 21 13:23 ..
drwxr-xr-x@   9 rundel  staff   288 Jan 21 13:22 .git
-rw-r--r--@   1 rundel  staff   109 Jan 21 13:22 .gitignore
-rw-r--r--@   1 rundel  staff     5 Jan 21 13:22 .python-version
-rw-r--r--@   1 rundel  staff    82 Jan 21 13:22 main.py
-rw-r--r--@   1 rundel  staff   150 Jan 21 13:22 pyproject.toml
-rw-r--r--@   1 rundel  staff     0 Jan 21 13:22 README.md
```
:::

. . .

This creates a `pyproject.toml`, a sample `main.py` script, and basic git infrastructure. Generally, we only really care about the `pyproject.toml` which we can exclusively generate via `uv init --bare`.


## `pyproject.toml`

Modern project metadata file, tracks python version and package dependencies among other details.

::: {.small}
```toml
[project]
name = "my-project"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Add your description here"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.14"
dependencies = []
```
:::



## Adding dependencies

Once we have our project setup we can add (and install) dependencies directly via uv. `uv add` updates `pyproject.toml` and installs the package (creating a venv if needed).

::: {.columns .xsmall}
::: {.column}
```bash
uv add numpy
```
```
Using CPython 3.14.2
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Resolved 2 packages in 157ms
Installed 1 package in 27ms
 + numpy==2.4.1
```
```bash
uv add pandas matplotlib scikit-learn
```
```
Resolved 18 packages in 490ms
Prepared 5 packages in 9.18s
Installed 15 packages in 134ms
 + contourpy==1.3.3
 + cycler==0.12.1
 + fonttools==4.61.1
 + joblib==1.5.3
 + kiwisolver==1.4.9
 + matplotlib==3.10.8
 + packaging==25.0
 + pandas==3.0.0
 + pillow==12.1.0
 + pyparsing==3.3.2
 + python-dateutil==2.9.0.post0
 + scikit-learn==1.8.0
 + scipy==1.17.0
 + six==1.17.0
 + threadpoolctl==3.6.0
```

:::
::: {.column .fragment}
```bash
uv add "pydantic<2"
```
```
Resolved 26 packages in 336ms
Prepared 1 package in 238ms
Installed 2 packages in 3ms
 + pydantic==1.10.26
 + typing-extensions==4.15.0
```
```bash
uv add --dev pytest ruff
```
```
Resolved 24 packages in 337ms
Prepared 4 packages in 859ms
Installed 5 packages in 27ms
 + iniconfig==2.3.0
 + pluggy==1.6.0
 + pygments==2.19.2
 + pytest==9.0.2
 + ruff==0.14.13
```
:::
::: 

## Updated `pyproject.toml`

::: {.small}
```toml
[project]
name = "my-project"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Add your description here"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.14"
dependencies = [
    "matplotlib>=3.10.8",
    "numpy>=2.4.1",
    "pandas>=3.0.0",
    "pydantic<2",
    "scikit-learn>=1.8.0",
]

[dependency-groups]
dev = [
    "pytest>=9.0.2",
    "ruff>=0.14.13",
]
```
:::


## Virtual environments

Virtual environments isolate project dependencies from the system Python and other projects. Packages are installed in a local folder in your project.

As we just saw, using `uv add` will create a new virtual environment in `.venv` by default if there is not an existing venv.

To explicitly create your own venv you can use,

::: {.small}
```bash
uv venv # Create a virtual environment
uv venv --python 3.13 # Or specify Python version
uv venv .venv # Or specify directory name
```
:::

## Activating environments

To use the virtual environment certain environmental variables need to be set correctly (e.g. `PATH`, `PYTHONPATH`, etc.) so that the correcy Python binary and libraries are used.

From the command line / terminal you can run the following in your project directory:

::: {.small}
```bash
source .venv/bin/activate  # macOS/Linux (bash/zsh)
.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1 # Windows (PowerShell)
.venv\Scripts\activate.bat # Windows (cmd)
```
:::

Alternatively (strongly suggested), use `uv run` to execute commands in the environment without activating,

::: {.small}
```bash
uv run python script.py
uv run pytest
uv quarto render test.qmd
```
:::


## uv and Positron

Positron automatically detects virtual environments in your project directory. When you open a folder containing a `.venv` directory (created by uv), Positron will:

* Detect the environment and offer to use it
* Show the active Python interpreter in the status bar
* Use the environment for the Python console and when running scripts

If not automatically detected, you can manually select the interpreter via the Command Palette (`Cmd+Shift+P` / `Ctrl+Shift+P`) and searching for "Python: Select Interpreter".


## `uv sync`

Since the `.venv` folder is system specific (and large) it is not typically commited to git. Instead you will likely clone a respository that just has a `pyproject.toml` file.

Use `uv sync` is used to construct the venv and install all dependencies for the project 

::: {.small}
```bash
uv sync
```
```
Using CPython 3.14.2
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Resolved 26 packages in 8ms
Installed 23 packages in 96ms
 + contourpy==1.3.3
 + cycler==0.12.1
 + fonttools==4.61.1
 + iniconfig==2.3.0
 + joblib==1.5.3
 + kiwisolver==1.4.9
 + matplotlib==3.10.8
 + numpy==2.4.1
 + packaging==25.0
 + pandas==3.0.0
 + pillow==12.1.0
 + pluggy==1.6.0
 + pydantic==1.10.26
 + pygments==2.19.2
 + pyparsing==3.3.2
 + pytest==9.0.2
 + python-dateutil==2.9.0.post0
 + ruff==0.14.13
 + scikit-learn==1.8.0
 + scipy==1.17.0
 + six==1.17.0
 + threadpoolctl==3.6.0
 + typing-extensions==4.15.0
```
:::





## Listing packages

::: {.small}
```bash
uv pip list
```
```
Package           Version
----------------- -----------
contourpy         1.3.3
cycler            0.12.1
fonttools         4.61.1
...
```

```bash
uv pip show numpy
```
```
Name: numpy
Version: 2.4.1
Location: /Users/rundel/my-project/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages
Requires:
Required-by: contourpy, matplotlib, pandas, scikit-learn, scipy
```
:::

`uv pip install` and `uv pip freeze` are also available as lower-level commands for working outside of a project context or with `requirements.txt` files.


## Dependency tree

::: {.xsmall}
```bash
uv tree
```
```
Resolved 26 packages in 8ms
my-project v0.1.0
├── matplotlib v3.10.8
│   ├── contourpy v1.3.3
│   │   └── numpy v2.4.1
│   ├── cycler v0.12.1
│   ├── fonttools v4.61.1
│   ├── kiwisolver v1.4.9
│   ├── numpy v2.4.1
│   ├── packaging v25.0
│   ├── pillow v12.1.0
│   ├── pyparsing v3.3.2
│   └── python-dateutil v2.9.0.post0
│       └── six v1.17.0
├── numpy v2.4.1
├── pandas v3.0.0
│   ├── numpy v2.4.1
│   └── python-dateutil v2.9.0.post0 (*)
├── pydantic v1.10.26
│   └── typing-extensions v4.15.0
├── scikit-learn v1.8.0
│   ├── joblib v1.5.3
│   ├── numpy v2.4.1
│   ├── scipy v1.17.0
│   │   └── numpy v2.4.1
│   └── threadpoolctl v3.6.0
├── pytest v9.0.2 (group: dev)
│   ├── iniconfig v2.3.0
│   ├── packaging v25.0
│   ├── pluggy v1.6.0
│   └── pygments v2.19.2
└── ruff v0.14.13 (group: dev)
(*) Package tree already displayed
```
:::


## Common workflows

New project setup:

::: {.small}
```bash
mkdir my-project && cd my-project
uv init --bare
uv add pandas numpy
uv run python project.py
```
:::

. . .

<br/>

Clone existing project:

::: {.small}
```bash
git clone <repo-url> && cd <repo>
uv sync
uv run python script.py
```
:::




## Exercise 2

::: {.code-file .sourceCode .cell-code}
`r fontawesome::fa("file")` &nbsp; `calculate_ci.py`
:::
::: {.small}
```{python}
#| eval: false
#| file: Lec05_notes/calculate_ci.py
```
:::

The script above computes a confidence interval but requires `numpy` and `scipy`.

1. Create a new directory and copy the script into it
2. Initialize a uv project and the required dependencies
3. Check that you are able to successfully run the script and see the calculation results
